1: direct Transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart; there is no fear of God before his eyes. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 3:18 as the climax of his catena of OT passages proving universal sin: 'There is no fear of God before their eyes.' The conclusion of Romans 3:10-18 sets up the need for Christ's justifying work — no one is exempt from this indictment, which is why Christ must be the righteousness of God for all who believe.
2: theme For he flatters himself in his own eyes that his iniquity cannot be found out and hated. Self-deception is the universal condition from which Christ liberates: humanity became futile in their thinking, and their foolish hearts were darkened (Romans 1:21). The renewal of the mind Christ brings (Romans 12:2) is the cure for self-flattering blindness.
3: theme The words of his mouth are trouble and deceit; he has ceased to act wisely and do good. This anatomy of verbal sin connects to Paul's portrait of universal corruption (Romans 3:13-14 similarly catalogs mouth-sins). Christ bears this indictment in the atonement — he who had no deceit in his mouth (1 Peter 2:22; Isaiah 53:9) bore the deceit of others.
4: shadow He plots trouble while on his bed; he sets himself in a way that is not good; he does not reject evil. The anatomy of sin becomes the frame for understanding those who plotted against Christ: the chief priests and elders of the people gathered in the palace of the high priest and plotted together to arrest Jesus by stealth and kill him (Matthew 26:3-4). Bedside plotting against the righteous one.
5: revelation of God Your steadfast love, O LORD, extends to the heavens; your faithfulness to the clouds. The cosmic chesed that spans the heavens finds its supreme revelation in the Incarnation and atonement: God's love is great — even when we were dead in our trespasses, [he] made us alive together with Christ (Ephesians 2:4-5). The love that reaches the heavens stooped to earth.
6: revelation of God Your righteousness is like the mountains of God; your judgments are like the great deep. The righteousness of God, firm as mountains and fathomless as the deep, is the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe (Romans 3:21-22). The mountain-firm righteousness becomes gift — imputed to the ungodly in Christ.
7: revelation of God How precious is your steadfast love, O God! The children of mankind take refuge in the shadow of your wings. The shadow of God's wings as refuge reaches its incarnate expression when Christ laments: How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing! (Matthew 23:37). The shadow of God's wings becomes the shadow of the cross.
8: shadow They feast on the abundance of your house, and you give them drink from the river of your delights. The eucharistic feast anticipation: Christ feeds the redeemed from the abundance of God's house (John 6:35 — whoever comes to me shall not hunger). The river of delights is the living water Christ gives (John 4:14; Revelation 22:1 — the river of the water of life).
9: direct For with you is the fountain of life; in your light do we see light. This verse is a proto-Johannine confession that John explicitly develops. In him was life, and the life was the light of men (John 1:4) — life and light united in Christ. I am the light of the world (John 8:12). The water I give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life (John 4:14). Christ is the fountain; Christ is the light.
10: theme Oh, continue your steadfast love to those who know you, and your righteousness to the upright of heart! The prayer for persevering love is answered in the unchanging Christ: Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever (Hebrews 13:8). He who loved his own to the end (John 13:1) continues his steadfast love through the intercession of the risen Lord (Romans 8:34).
11: shadow Let not the foot of arrogance come upon me, nor the hand of the wicked drive me away. Those who trample underfoot the Son of God face the severest judgment (Hebrews 10:29 — how much worse punishment will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God). The righteous sufferer's prayer against being trampled is answered by Christ's exaltation above all rule and authority (Ephesians 1:21).
12: shadow There the evildoers lie fallen; they are thrust down, unable to rise. The proleptic defeat of all who oppose Christ — the Book of Revelation's vision of the final overthrow: the beast was captured, and with it the false prophet (Revelation 19:20). Those who were unable to fall before the cross find themselves unable to rise at the judgment of the one who was himself raised.
